original_abstract: Border deaths, also referred to as migrant deaths or deaths during migration, are premature deaths of individuals whose movement and/or presence in a certain territory has been irregularised. Data on border deaths, presented separately or alongside figures on border length, the number of border crossings, irregular crossings, asylum seekers, or smugglers, are often part of the repertoire of contemporary media representations of irregularised migration. As such, they can be also mobilised in perpetuating narratives of “crisis” and reinforcing the perception of certain mobilities as illegal and dangerous, and even in normalising death as an inevitable element of irregularised migration. In sharp contrast with the media representations, data on the deceased and disappeared at borders remain invisible in official statistics. Since states do not record in official documents whether a deceased individual was a migrant or if the death is related to migration, deceased refugees and other migrants are “invisibly” included in post-mortem administrative procedures, and thus in official mortality statistics – assuming those statistics even account for non-citizens or unidentified persons. Consequently, local actors, including municipal cemetery services, hospitals, police, and activists, have taken the initiative to document these deaths. Some do so out of professional duty, while others respond to a perceived moral obligation to preserve records. However, motives vary widely, and systematic state-level documentation efforts only emerged in response to heightened media attention. The oldest known initiative for documenting deaths at borders, the List of Refugee Deaths by the United for Intercultural Action (UNITED) network, was initiated in the 1990s in the context of reconfiguring and hardening border controls in post-Cold War Europe, the internationalisation of the European anti-racist movement, and the turn to metrics in the field of human rights. UNITED list is regularly updated and published in a revised edition on an annual basis. The list aims to highlight the systemic failures of European migration policies that endanger lives. It draws attention to a wide range of deaths tied to irregular migration, including those occurring in detention centres, during deportation etc. UNITED’s approach emphasises the importance of acknowledging every death while recognising structural violence, clearly positioning border deaths as lethal outcomes of current migration policies, or deaths by policy. Despite systematic efforts by UNITED to systematically document and disseminate data on border deaths, information from this area seemed to reach the broader public only after the major shipwreck near Lampedusa in October 2013. In response to the shipwreck, the International Organization for Migration (IOM), known for its project-oriented approach and constant search for new operational niches, began collecting and soon distributing data on border deaths. As already highlighted in literature, the methodological focus of the IOM project on deaths in transit, antismuggling advocacy, and its geographic emphasis on the Mediterranean results in selective representations that obscure the state origins of violence i.e. state responsi bilities for border deaths. Unlike the UNITED list, which politicised border deaths by positioning them as a catalyst for a broader struggle against racism, the IOM project, despite shifts in the last ten years of its existence, is inclined towards their naturalisation and humanitarianisation, aligning also in this area with the demands of states and donors. From this perspective, the IOM project could be related to the growing migration industry and its constant demand for new activities and niches, on the one hand, and, on the other, the humanitarianisation of borders, rooted in the fusion of care and control, or the care-control nexus. In spite of their differences, both UNITED’s and IOM’s projects call for reflection and raise questions about the game-changing effects of documenting border deaths and data-driven advocacy. Globally, as well as in Croatia, where the numbers of those killed and displaced in the 1990es war – and even in World War II – remain among the most sensitive (daily) political topics, the numbers of border deaths are not subject to dispute. In fact, the opposite seems to be happening: there is a conflict-free circulation of various, often contradictory, and hard-to-compare numbers from different sources and methodologies that blend in media reports and public statements into an amorphous mass of figures surrounding knowledge of irregular migration and border deaths. Moreover, while even the slightest rise in asylum seeker numbers or irregular crossings readily prompts at least nominal responses like reintroduction of border controls in the Schengen Area, the continuous rise in border deaths does not provoke remotely similar, concrete, and prompt reactions. Starting with this observation, the article brings this discussion to artistic, feminist, decolonial, and abolitionist critical approaches to documenting racially coded violence and border deaths. Is it enough to include the excluded into the existing order? Or is it necessary, as Judith Butler argues, to return to the questions: Whose lives are considered lives? Whose lives are deemed unreal, and whose real? Whose lives are grievable, and whose are not? The question is not simply about the presence or absence of violence, but about whose lives are made to matter. And, what if documentation efforts focused on deaths at borders align with, rather than challenge dominant order, policies, and practices of border control, degradation, and scaling of people? Can practices of documenting violence serve as relevant factors and effective agents of change in a context where border violence is tolerated when racially coded, where it is an integral part of, rather than an aberration from, the border regime? Is documenting and presenting data about violence, especially when decontextualised, reducing victims of violence to objects of the spectacle of violence, while simultaneously reinforcing the superior position of researchers and activists as those who are in a position to condemn and expose that violence? Scarce or almost non-existent socially transformative effects of documentation efforts focused on border deaths – even those that emerged within broader social movements and struggles, like the UNITED List of Refugee Deaths – empirically support the idea that this type of data could align with dominant social relations and administrative procedures. In this vein, the IOM project, as an example of the appropriation and even commodification of activist practices – since it involves the transfer of knowledge and procedures from activist struggles and movements into the migration industry – can be seen as supporting the thesis of the (potentially) active role of documentation efforts in sustaining existing social inequalities, systems of control, and dehumanisation. However, the question remains as to what extent presented problems and perspectives apply to other, more grassroots and vernacular documentation efforts by survivors, families, and activist groups, which emphasise the egalitarian and affective dimensions of documenting border deaths. These efforts and the related affects of grief and resentment could be seen as potential triggers for struggles for alternative values and, in terms of Clare Hemmings, affective solidarity. This solidarity is not based on shared identity or experience, but on a sense of “desire for transformation born out of the experience of discomfort and against all odds” (Hemmings, 2012: 158). It is created from anger and frustration, and sustained through mourning and connection, in which documentation practices can also play a role.
humanized_abstract: Border deaths or migrant deaths or deaths during migration are premature deaths occurring to migrants or asylum seekers whose movement and/or presence in a particular border area has been irregularised. Most of the time, data on border deaths are reported alongside the total length of borders, or alongside statistics on border crossings, irregular entries, asylum seekers, or even people smuggling. This way these reports come to contribute to the media coverage of irregular migration and to reinforce negative images of migrants and thus reproduce representations of ‘abnormal’ migration as a ‘crisis’. However, reports on deaths and disappearances of migrants at borders remain almost invisible in official statistics. This is because states do not include such data in their official records, because deaths of migrants or asylum seekers are not registered in official death or forensic records as ‘migrant deaths’ or as deaths related to migration, which means that post-mortem procedures and data included in mortality statistics therefore do not record that refugees and migrants have died (presupposing that non-citizen deaths and unidentified bodies are included in the official mortality statistics at all). Although mortality of migrants and refugees at borders is invisible to the states concerned, and therefore to their statistics, there are several non-state initiatives documenting these deaths, carried out from motives that vary from civic duty through a feeling of moral obligation to record these deaths for the sake of memory, to a politics of grief. Documenting border deaths is a relatively recent endeavour, that primarily originated among local non-governmental stakeholders and social movements. State-level documentation of border deaths as a data collection activity only started when coupled with intense media reporting on the issue. The List of Refugee Deaths started being recorded by the network of NGOs united within United for Intercultural Action (UNITED) project in the 1990s when the Cold War borders were transformed into more severe apparatuses by bringing the newly established international anti-racist movement together with new human rights metrics. UNITED updates and regularly releases a List of Refugee Deaths the newest edition of which is published annually. The List aspires to make European migration policies more transparent to their deadly effects by documenting a wide range of deaths happening to refugees due to their migration or status as migrants or refugees (such as deaths in detention centres, during deportations etc.) and to express them as structural murders, or deaths by policy. It aspires to bring into focus every death on its list, document structural violence and express the deaths at the borders as deadly effects of migration policies and their apparatuses. It was only after the major shipwreck at Lampedusa in October 2013 that IOM started to collect and report border deaths, taking in focus the deaths in transit through countries as a result of smuggling activities. The IOM project includes also deaths at borders of irregular border-crossers but only records deaths at the Mediterranean border that occurred due to the action of smugglers on travellers and which could therefore be seen as an additional negative effect of illicit people smuggling. As the IOM reports, although the IOM project has gone through a few format and terminology changes in the last ten years it predominantly presents deaths in transit as a ‘human cost’ of smuggling activities, an ‘add-on’ to the primary smuggling business and hence to a considerable degree downplaying the role of deaths related to state control of borders. This focus, its metrics, terms and therefore the selective view that this data brings upon border deaths and their state-related character differs substantially from that of the UNITED list focusing in more inclusive manner on structural violence of borders, its apparatuses, migration policies and their murders-as-politics (Mikelić Prerun, 2014, 2015), the effect of borders and migration policies on refugees and their right to life – focusing deaths by policy as killings at the border of those whose lives or deaths are deemed less significant to be registered, counted, known. Moreover, it differs from the UNITED list in a more significant manner, since the IOM as part of the growing migration industry is also engaged in transforming the activists' work on bringing deaths into record to data, therefore contributing to the humanitarianisation of borders and thereby strengthening the care-control nexus which simultaneously produces an ideology that deaths at borders are unavoidable in case of the migrant or refugee, while others including also local stakeholders who have to record these deaths because no official authority or body will do so appear to perpetuate them as an unproblematic part of their business and part of border management. Despite obvious and highly visible discrepancies between the UNITED list and the IOM project documenting deaths at borders, a discourse related to border deaths as a subject that calls for humanitarian action as part of a migration industry including multiple and various forms of humanitarianism and care-control is on the rise at borders and beyond. Such use and appropriation of data and activism by the migration industry leads to documenting and data-driven advocacy on the fatal aspect of borders to potentially reinforce the very border apparatus and its system of dehumanisation and fatalities; especially if these projects reinforce the current dominant representations of fatal borders in society, including those of migration-related or migration-linked fatal border events as being unavoidable or in any case imposable to change. One therefore should reflect upon whether documenting border deaths and data-driven activism for improving mortality rates at borders has changed these facts and attitudes, and what the game-changing potential of these data could be. While the fatalism that currently characterises border deaths seems to be one of the factors dissolving public interest in mortification of borders – which has not yet led to concrete changes in state's borders management and control regardless of increasing mortality of migrants and refugees at borders – as has already been demonstrated by critical studies on spectacle, affects and deaths in the border regime, it remains unclear whether mortality data, which are already far from few and rarely disputed in the case of borders, are in fact used as an important factor that determines possible change or as an agent of change in border politics and regimes as long as border violence in considered legitimate due to it being racially coded, part of border control, while also being a normal, operational component of borders. While data and documentation of border deaths have apparently scarce socially transformative effects – irrespective of these being recorded by activist movements and social movements in general, or if the deaths are recorded also as part of wider movements and social actions (such as with the UNITED list of Refugee Deaths), all information around potential transformative power of data on border deaths empirically points to documentation projects along the lines of mortality and mortality-related data that have been carried out so far contributing to border deaths remaining an unproblematic phenomenon and continuing to reproduce current social relations and control measures at borders including administrative procedures related to registering human death and recording and registering mortality rates. This includes an example of what I term the appropriation and potential commodities of activist knowledge and practices, such as in the case of data related to border deaths in relation to the IOM project. However, this is an example related more to activism and social movement documenting death in a particular way and its appropriation by an organisation that also commodity data related to migration by acting it out in a border regime as part of a larger migration industry which promotes and includes humanitarian metrics; the commodities and market of migration industry that is already diverse and wide; whereas more grassroots and affect-centred and solidarity-related aspects of documenting deaths at borders have not yet been researched. It is indeed crucial that one questions how data-driven activism and its transformative potential on deaths in the border regime and fatalisation of borders functions where grassroots activism of families of the dead, activist organisations or migrants themselves record these deaths documenting the gruesome character of deaths and the violence behind it, thereby engaging also in a politics of grief documenting affectively these deaths and expressing their resentment, mourning and feelings for those lost in borders or rather expressing a wish to change borders through their grief and empathy. Practices related to documentation of deaths such as activism around victims and mourning of those lost at borders relate to what, inspired by feminist, decolonial and abolitionist critical perspectives on violence and its documentation, I would call affective solidarity building beyond citizenship, beyond humanity and beyond life. Inspired by feminist and affective politics Judith Butler (Butler 2010, 2016) also reflects upon the transformative aspect of politics of grief of death when non-human lives and deaths are not considered human. Butler brings our attention to the fact that killing someone means ending lives, but that the term lives and deaths implies values assigned to them. They pose important questions in relation to border deaths such as Whose lives are lives? Whose lives or deaths are considered to be unreal? Whose lives or deaths are therefore not real? Whose lives are thus grievable? And whose lives or deaths are not? Thus whereas Butler brings our attention to how data about victims or deaths may not be considered lives in the case of those that are killed because their status or existence has been deemed non-human, we here add another element by highlighting that the impact and documentation of their deaths can also be non-transformative or even potentially aligning with the current regime. However, a major question remains whether these aspects relate also to activism or activism practices and their social transformations with regards to the borders and their fatalisation where it is not carried out among citizens of countries having borders and their related national identities but among families of victims, or their representatives and among migrants. One question remains – as has also been highlighted by studies relating to victim activism around border deaths and mortification at borders more generally – whether these practices and data, which focus in equal measure on the rights to exist, grievability of victims and their deaths and at the same time potentially on the non-changeable status of fatalisation of borders, can therefore act as a catalysts of social change in favour of victimising deaths and mortification of borders, when this activism focuses less on citizen rights and mortality figures related to citizenship, but rather on affective documentation and solidarity related to victimisation of deaths including feelings for those deceased.
